titleStmt.title: Letter from Sir Wilfrid Spender to his wife Lady Lillian Spender, 9 July 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Wilfrid Bliss Spender

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: A letter from sir Wilfrid Bliss Spender (1876-1960) to his wife, Lady Lillian Spender, née Dean, (1880-1968). In this letter Spender writes that he will be moving to a new position the next day. He also briefly mentions Ulster politics, his mother's health, and the generous positivity of The Times' representation of the Ulster Division. He revels in the thought of Lillian 'on the seashore with Sredward (Edward Carson) and Lady C, getting pinker and pinker in the hot sun'.Sir Wilfrid Spender was a member of the British Army until 1913 when controversy surrounding his signature of the Ulster Solemn League and Covenant resulted in his resignation. Spender was called up for military service in 1914 and from 1915 served in the 36th (Ulster) Division. Throughout the war, Spender wrote a collection of letters to his wife Lady Lillian Spender. The letters date mainly from 1915 to 1918, when Sir Wilfrid was serving in France. The letters concern politics, the Irish question, personal life, and Wilfrid's experience of the Great War. Lady Lillian served in the Ulster Volunteer Nursing Corps during the war, and was an organiser of the Ulster Division Comforts Fund.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
9July My Dear Lil A perfect day & we seem
miles away from the events of
last week. The letters from
home help to complete the
dil> illusion. Elsie's letters show
immense pleasure in her
work. I'm so glad. Singleton & I leave early
tomorrow for a new destination.
We hope it only means that
the Division is being taken
up somewhere to reorganize,
& that we shall again return
to the more pleasant country
where we now are. I have just read the MP
leader - Friday's - which must
be unpleasant reading to BL,
because every word of it rings
out it truth. In this world
the honest man - such as I
still believe BL to be - has
no chance against the Squiffy
crew. I am not writing to
Burlington tho' I think of
you on the sea shore with Siredward
& Lady C, getting pinker &
pinker I the hot sun. It is wonderful to me how
Mater has stood the past
week so well . I think she
feels that the responsibility is
on you, & if you manage the all
right she can. The Times has certainly let
itself go over the Ulster Div-
far more than the MP . Perhaps
it feels some qualms & hopes
to soothe them.
It is very curious that
the Times should have
been generous in its praise
& not the MP, but the
latter is probably waiting
for details. I hope so any-
how or I shall be greatly
disappointed. Perhaps one of
the 2 families will have
kept the cuttings in Tuesday
& Wednesday's Times &
I may see the Belfast
Whig too which is sent to
Henry.
